title: Tiled Matrix Multiplication
description: Build matrix multiplication from scratch on the GPU: start with a naive kernel, analyze its memory bottleneck, then implement the tiled algorithm using shared memory for dramatic speedups. The cornerstone of GPU computing.

sections.title: Why Matmul Matters
sections.content:
Matrix multiplication is not just an academic exercise -- it is the single most important operation in modern computing. It is the core of:

- **Deep learning:** Every dense layer, attention mechanism, and convolution reduces to matrix multiplication
- **Scientific computing:** Linear algebra, simulations, finite element methods
- **Computer graphics:** Transformations, projections, lighting calculations
- **Signal processing:** FFTs, filtering, correlation

A single forward pass through GPT-3 (175B parameters) performs roughly **350 trillion** multiply-accumulate operations, almost entirely matrix multiplications. The entire field of GPU computing exists largely because GPUs are extraordinarily good at matrix multiplication.

> **Cross-reference:** This module builds directly on Module 3's introduction to shared memory. If you are not comfortable with `cuda.shared.array`, `syncthreads()`, and the concept of block-level cooperation, review Module 3 first. Everything here depends on those foundations.

### The Algorithm

To multiply matrix A (M x K) by matrix B (K x N) to produce matrix C (M x N):

```
C[i][j] = sum over k of A[i][k] * B[k][j]    for k = 0, 1, ..., K-1
```

Each element of C is the **dot product** of row i from A and column j from B:

```
         B (K x N)
         [ b00 b01 b02 ]
         [ b10 b11 b12 ]
         [ b20 b21 b22 ]
         [ b30 b31 b32 ]

A (M x K)                    C (M x N)
[ a00 a01 a02 a03 ]         [ c00 c01 c02 ]
[ a10 a11 a12 a13 ]    =    [ c10 c11 c12 ]

c01 = a00*b01 + a01*b11 + a02*b21 + a03*b31
    = dot(A[row=0], B[col=1])
```

### Naive GPU Implementation

The most straightforward GPU implementation assigns **one thread per output element**. Each thread computes one element of C by iterating through the corresponding row of A and column of B:

```python
from numba import cuda
import numpy as np

@cuda.jit
def naive_matmul(A, B, C):
    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    if row < C.shape[0] and col < C.shape[1]:
        total = 0.0
        for k in range(A.shape[1]):
            total += A[row, k] * B[k, col]
        C[row, col] = total
```

This kernel is correct and simple. But it has a devastating performance problem -- which we will dissect in the next section.

sections.title: The Memory Problem
sections.content:
### Memory Access Analysis: The Problem Let us analyze the memory accesses for a 1024x1024 matrix multiplication: **Total output elements:** 1024 x 1024 = 1,048,576 (each computed by one thread) **Work per thread:** Each thread performs K=1024 multiply-add operations, reading one element from A and one from B per iteration. So each thread does: - 1024 reads from A (one full row) - 1024 reads from B (one full column) - 1 write to C - Total: 2048 reads + 1 write per thread **Total global memory reads:** 1,048,576 threads x 2,048 reads = **2.1 billion reads** But wait -- how much **unique** data is there? - A has 1024 x 1024 = 1,048,576 elements - B has 1024 x 1024 = 1,048,576 elements - Total unique data: ~2.1 million elements = ~8 MB We are reading 8 MB of unique data but performing **8.6 billion** bytes of global memory reads. Each element is read, on average, **1024 times**. This is a **1024x redundancy** in memory access. ### Visualizing the Redundancy The diagram below shows how threads in the same block row all need the same row of A, and threads in the same block column all need the same column of B: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph row0["Threads computing row 0 of C"] direction TB T00["Thread(0,0) -> C[0,0]
reads A[0,:] + B[:,0]"] T01["Thread(0,1) -> C[0,1]
reads A[0,:] + B[:,1]"] T02["Thread(0,2) -> C[0,2]
reads A[0,:] + B[:,2]"] end subgraph col0["Threads computing col 0 of C"] direction TB T10["Thread(1,0) -> C[1,0]
reads A[1,:] + B[:,0]"] T20["Thread(2,0) -> C[2,0]
reads A[2,:] + B[:,0]"] end Aredundant["Row 0 of A read by ALL 1024 threads in row 0 of C
= 1024x redundancy"] Bredundant["Col 0 of B read by ALL 1024 threads in col 0 of C
= 1024x redundancy"] row0 --> Aredundant col0 --> Bredundant classDef bad fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca class Aredundant,Bredundant bad ``` All threads in row 0 of C read **the same row of A** (row 0). That is 1024 threads all reading the same 1024 elements -- 1024x redundancy for A. Similarly, all threads in column 0 of C read **the same column of B** (column 0). Another 1024x redundancy. > **The fundamental insight:** In naive matrix multiplication, each element of A is read N times (once for each column of C in that row), and each element of B is read M times (once for each row of C in that column). This is enormously wasteful when reads go to slow global memory. ### Think Like a GPU: You Are Thread (3, 5) in Block (1, 2) Let us walk through exactly what happens for one specific thread in the naive kernel with TILE_SIZE=16: ``` You are thread (tx=5, ty=3) in block (bx=1, by=2). Your output position: row = by * 16 + ty = 2 * 16 + 3 = 35 col = bx * 16 + tx = 1 * 16 + 5 = 21 You compute C[35, 21]. Your work in the naive kernel: for k = 0, 1, 2, ..., 1023: total += A[35, k] * B[k, 21] That is 1024 reads from row 35 of A (global memory) + 1024 reads from col 21 of B (global memory) = 2048 global memory reads for ONE output element. Meanwhile, thread (tx=6, ty=3) in your same block reads the EXACT same row 35 of A. That is 1024 reads that are identical to yours. All 16 threads in your block row read that same row of A. If only you could share those reads... (That is exactly what tiling does -- see next section.) ```

sections.title: Naive Performance & Coalescing
sections.content:
### Performance of the Naive Kernel

On a T4 GPU with 1024x1024 float32 matrices:
- **Theoretical peak:** ~8.1 TFLOPS (FP32)
- **Naive kernel:** ~0.1-0.3 TFLOPS
- **Utilization:** ~2-4% of peak

Here are real benchmark numbers showing how the naive kernel performs across matrix sizes:

| Size | Time (ms) | GFLOPS | % of T4 Peak (8100 GFLOPS) |
|------|-----------|--------|----------------------------|
| 256x256   | 0.3  | 112 | 1.4% |
| 512x512   | 1.8  | 149 | 1.8% |
| 1024x1024 | 12.5 | 172 | 2.1% |
| 2048x2048 | 95.0 | 181 | 2.2% |

The naive kernel achieves only 2-4% of the GPU's compute capability because it is **completely memory-bound**. The GPU's compute units are starving -- they can multiply numbers 100x faster than the memory system can deliver them.

### The Compute-to-Memory Ratio

The key metric is the **compute-to-global-memory-access ratio** (also called operational intensity or arithmetic intensity). This concept will become central in Module 6 when we study the roofline model:

```
Naive matmul:
  FLOPs per element of C: 2K (K multiply-adds)
  Global memory reads per element of C: 2K (K from A + K from B)
  Bytes read per element of C: 2K * 4 = 8K bytes
  Ratio: 2K / 8K = 0.25 FLOPs/byte
```

The T4 GPU has:
- Compute: 8.1 TFLOPS
- Memory bandwidth: 320 GB/s
- Balance point: 8100 / 320 = **25.3 FLOPs/byte**

Our naive kernel achieves 0.25 FLOPs/byte, which is **100x below the balance point**. We need to increase the ratio by loading less data from global memory -- which means **reusing** data that has already been loaded.

This is exactly what the tiling strategy achieves, and we will explore it next.

> **Quick check:** If matrices are 2048x2048, how many times is each element of A read from global memory in the naive kernel? What is the total number of global memory reads across all threads? (Answer: N=2048 times each; total = 2048^2 threads x 2x2048 reads = ~17 billion reads.)

### The Coalescing Problem in B

There is an additional problem specific to how B is accessed. Recall that in row-major memory layout, elements in the same row are contiguous. When a thread reads `B[k, col]` for varying k (the inner loop), it is reading down a **column** of B. Consecutive threads (varying col) read `B[k, 0]`, `B[k, 1]`, `B[k, 2]`, etc. -- these ARE consecutive in memory, so the read is coalesced.

But when k advances (next iteration), each thread reads `B[k+1, col]` -- jumping by N floats in memory. This means the access pattern within a single thread is strided, though across threads it is coalesced for a given k.

The tiled approach solves both the redundancy and the access pattern issues by loading tiles into fast shared memory.
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda

# ============================================
# Naive Matrix Multiplication
# ============================================

@cuda.jit
def naive_matmul(A, B, C):
    """Each thread computes one element of C."""
    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    if row < C.shape[0] and col < C.shape[1]:
        total = 0.0
        for k in range(A.shape[1]):
            total += A[row, k] * B[k, col]
        C[row, col] = total

# Test correctness first with small matrices
print("=== Correctness Test ===")
M, K, N = 64, 128, 96
A = np.random.randn(M, K).astype(np.float32)
B = np.random.randn(K, N).astype(np.float32)
C_expected = A @ B

d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
d_B = cuda.to_device(B)
d_C = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)

TPB = (16, 16)
BPG = ((N + TPB[0] - 1) // TPB[0], (M + TPB[1] - 1) // TPB[1])

naive_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C)
C_gpu = d_C.copy_to_host()

assert np.allclose(C_gpu, C_expected, atol=1e-3), "Results mismatch!"
print(f"  {M}x{K} @ {K}x{N} = {M}x{N}: PASSED")

# Benchmark at different sizes
print("\n=== Naive Matmul Benchmark ===")
sizes = [256, 512, 1024, 2048]

for size in sizes:
    M = K = N = size
    A = np.random.randn(M, K).astype(np.float32)
    B = np.random.randn(K, N).astype(np.float32)

    d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
    d_B = cuda.to_device(B)
    d_C = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)

    BPG = ((N + TPB[0] - 1) // TPB[0], (M + TPB[1] - 1) // TPB[1])

    # Warm up
    naive_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C)
    cuda.synchronize()

    # Benchmark
    iters = 10 if size <= 1024 else 3
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        naive_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C)
    cuda.synchronize()
    gpu_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    # Calculate GFLOPS: 2*M*N*K FLOPs for matmul
    flops = 2 * M * N * K
    gflops = flops / gpu_time / 1e9

    # Compare with NumPy
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        C_cpu = A @ B
    cpu_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    print(f"  {size}x{size}: GPU={gpu_time*1000:.1f}ms ({gflops:.0f} GFLOPS), "
          f"NumPy={cpu_time*1000:.1f}ms, Speedup={cpu_time/gpu_time:.1f}x")

    # Verify correctness
    assert np.allclose(d_C.copy_to_host(), A @ B, atol=1e-2 * size/256)

print("\nNote: Naive matmul achieves very low GFLOPS because it is")
print("memory-bound. Each element of A and B is loaded from global")
print("memory K times. The tiled version in the next section fixes this.")

# ============================================
# Memory Access Analysis
# ============================================
print("\n=== Memory Access Analysis (1024x1024) ===")
size = 1024
total_output_elements = size * size
reads_per_element = 2 * size  # K reads from A + K reads from B
total_reads = total_output_elements * reads_per_element
unique_elements = 2 * size * size
redundancy = total_reads / unique_elements

print(f"  Output elements: {total_output_elements:,}")
print(f"  Global reads per element: {reads_per_element:,}")
print(f"  Total global reads: {total_reads:,} ({total_reads * 4 / 1e9:.1f} GB)")
print(f"  Unique data: {unique_elements:,} elements ({unique_elements * 4 / 1e6:.1f} MB)")
print(f"  Redundancy: {redundancy:.0f}x (each element read {redundancy:.0f} times!)")
print(f"  Arithmetic intensity: {2*size / (2*size*4):.2f} FLOPs/byte")
print(f"  T4 balance point: ~25 FLOPs/byte")
print(f"  -> We are {25 / (2*size / (2*size*4)):.0f}x below the balance point!")

sections.title: The Tiling Insight
sections.content:
The naive kernel's problem is clear: each element of A and B is loaded from slow global memory hundreds or thousands of times. The solution is equally clear: **load each element once into fast shared memory, then reuse it from there.**

But shared memory is small -- typically 48 KB per block (as you learned in Module 3). For a 1024x1024 matrix, A alone takes 4 MB. We cannot fit even one matrix in shared memory.

The solution is **tiling**: break the computation into small tiles that fit in shared memory, process them one at a time, and accumulate partial results.

### How Tiling Works

Consider computing one element C[row, col]. It requires the dot product of A's row `row` and B's column `col`:

```
C[row, col] = A[row, 0]*B[0, col] + A[row, 1]*B[1, col] + ... + A[row, K-1]*B[K-1, col]
```

We can split this sum into chunks of size TILE_SIZE:

```
C[row, col] = (A[row, 0]*B[0, col] + ... + A[row, T-1]*B[T-1, col])     # Phase 0
            + (A[row, T]*B[T, col] + ... + A[row, 2T-1]*B[2T-1, col])   # Phase 1
            + ...                                                        # Phase 2...
            + (A[row, (P-1)*T]*B[(P-1)*T, col] + ...)                    # Phase P-1
```

where T = TILE_SIZE and P = K / TILE_SIZE phases.

In each phase, we only need a **TILE_SIZE-wide strip** of A and a **TILE_SIZE-tall strip** of B. These strips are small enough to fit in shared memory.

> **Quick check:** In a tiled matmul with TILE=16 and matrices 1024x1024, how many phases will there be? How many global loads does each block do compared to the naive kernel? (Answer: 1024/16 = 64 phases. Each block performs 16x fewer global loads than naive -- each tile element is loaded once into shared memory and then reused TILE_SIZE=16 times by the threads in that block.)

### The Algorithm, Step by Step

For a block of threads computing a TILE_SIZE x TILE_SIZE submatrix of C:

**Phase 0:**
1. All threads cooperatively load a TILE_SIZE x TILE_SIZE tile from A (rows corresponding to this block, columns 0..TILE_SIZE-1) into shared memory `As`
2. All threads cooperatively load a TILE_SIZE x TILE_SIZE tile from B (rows 0..TILE_SIZE-1, columns corresponding to this block) into shared memory `Bs`
3. `syncthreads()` -- ensure all loads are complete
4. Each thread computes a partial dot product using the tile: `sum += As[ty][k] * Bs[k][tx]` for k=0..TILE_SIZE-1
5. `syncthreads()` -- ensure all threads are done reading before we overwrite the tile

**Phase 1:**
1. Load the next tile from A (columns TILE_SIZE..2*TILE_SIZE-1) and B (rows TILE_SIZE..2*TILE_SIZE-1)
2. Sync, compute partial dot product, sync

**...repeat for all phases...**

**After all phases:** Write the accumulated sum to C[row, col]

sections.title: Tile Loading & Phases
sections.content:
### Tile Loading Pattern: Which Thread Loads What

This is the key mechanical detail. Each thread (ty, tx) in the block loads exactly one element into each shared memory tile. The mapping is direct:

```
Block (1, 0) computes C[0:16, 16:32].  TILE_SIZE = 16.

  Phase 0: load A[0:16, 0:16] and B[0:16, 16:32]
  ============================================================

  Thread (ty=0,tx=0) loads:  As[0][0] = A[ 0,  0]    Bs[0][0] = B[ 0, 16]
  Thread (ty=0,tx=1) loads:  As[0][1] = A[ 0,  1]    Bs[0][1] = B[ 0, 17]
  Thread (ty=0,tx=2) loads:  As[0][2] = A[ 0,  2]    Bs[0][2] = B[ 0, 18]
     ...                        ...                      ...
  Thread (ty=1,tx=0) loads:  As[1][0] = A[ 1,  0]    Bs[1][0] = B[ 1, 16]
  Thread (ty=3,tx=5) loads:  As[3][5] = A[ 3,  5]    Bs[3][5] = B[ 3, 21]
     ...                        ...                      ...
  Thread (ty=15,tx=15) loads: As[15][15]=A[15,15]    Bs[15][15]=B[15,31]

  => 256 threads load 256 elements of A and 256 elements of B.
     Total: 512 global memory reads (vs 256 x 2 x 1024 = 524,288 naive).

  Phase 1: load A[0:16, 16:32] and B[16:32, 16:32]
  ============================================================
  Thread (ty=0,tx=0) loads:  As[0][0] = A[ 0, 16]    Bs[0][0] = B[16, 16]
  Thread (ty=3,tx=5) loads:  As[3][5] = A[ 3, 21]    Bs[3][5] = B[19, 21]
     ...slides the window right along A, down along B...
```

### Phase Progression: The Sliding Window

Here is the big picture of how tiles slide across A and down B as phases progress (here `r = blockIdx.y * T` and `c = blockIdx.x * T` are the row/col origins of this block's output tile):

```cudadiagram
TileSlidingPhases
```

### Visualizing the Block-Level Tiling

```cudadiagram
MatmulTileDecomposition
```

sections.title: Thread's-Eye View & Memory Wins
sections.content:
### Think Like a GPU: You Are Thread (3, 5) in the Tiled Kernel

Let us revisit our thread from section 2, but now in the tiled kernel. Same block (1, 2), same thread (tx=5, ty=3):

```
You are thread (tx=5, ty=3) in block (bx=1, by=2), TILE=16.

Your output: C[35, 21] (same as before).

Phase 0:
  1. YOU load As[3][5] = A[35, 5]  and  Bs[3][5] = B[3, 21]
     (your 15 blockmates load the other 15x16-1 elements)
  2. syncthreads() -- wait for all 256 threads
  3. YOU compute: for k=0..15: total += As[3][k] * Bs[k][5]
     This uses A[35, 0..15] and B[0..15, 21] -- all from shared memory!
  4. syncthreads() -- ensure nobody overwrites As/Bs yet

Phase 1:
  1. YOU load As[3][5] = A[35, 21] and  Bs[3][5] = B[19, 21]
  2-4. Same sync-compute-sync pattern.

...64 phases total...

Finally: C[35, 21] = total

Your global memory reads: 64 phases x 2 loads = 128 reads
Naive version:           2048 reads
Reduction: 16x (= TILE_SIZE)
```

### Hand-Traced Phase: A Tiny 4x4 Example

To make the mechanics fully concrete, let us trace one thread through two phases of a 4x4 @ 4x4 matmul with TILE_SIZE=2 (so each block is 2x2 threads, and there are 2 phases). Take **thread (ty=0, tx=1) in block (by=0, bx=0)**, computing C[0,1].

**Phase 0 (covers k=0..1):**
- Load step: this thread writes `As[0][1] = A[0,1]` and `Bs[0][1] = B[0,1]`. Its three blockmates load `A[0,0]`, `A[1,0]`, `A[1,1]` into As and `B[0,0]`, `B[1,0]`, `B[1,1]` into Bs.
- After `syncthreads()`, the 2x2 As tile holds `A[0:2, 0:2]` and Bs holds `B[0:2, 0:2]`.
- Compute step: `total += As[0][0]*Bs[0][1] + As[0][1]*Bs[1][1]` = `A[0,0]*B[0,1] + A[0,1]*B[1,1]`. That is the first half of the C[0,1] dot product.

**Phase 1 (covers k=2..3):**
- Load step: this thread writes `As[0][1] = A[0,3]` and `Bs[0][1] = B[2,1]`. The window has slid right along A (cols 2..3) and down along B (rows 2..3).
- After `syncthreads()`, As holds `A[0:2, 2:4]` and Bs holds `B[2:4, 0:2]`.
- Compute step: `total += As[0][0]*Bs[0][1] + As[0][1]*Bs[1][1]` = `A[0,2]*B[2,1] + A[0,3]*B[3,1]`. The remaining half.

**After both phases:** `total = A[0,0]*B[0,1] + A[0,1]*B[1,1] + A[0,2]*B[2,1] + A[0,3]*B[3,1]` = C[0,1]. The thread writes it. Notice it issued **4 global loads total** (2 phases x 2 loads), not 8 like the naive kernel would.

### Memory Access Improvement

Let us compare the memory access patterns:

**Naive (no tiling):**
- Each thread reads K elements from A and K elements from B
- Total global reads per block of TILE_SIZE^2 threads: TILE_SIZE^2 * 2K

**Tiled:**
- In each phase, the block loads TILE_SIZE^2 elements from A and TILE_SIZE^2 from B
- Over K/TILE_SIZE phases: (K/TILE_SIZE) * 2 * TILE_SIZE^2 = 2 * K * TILE_SIZE
- Total global reads per block: 2 * K * TILE_SIZE

**Reduction factor:**
```
Naive:  TILE_SIZE^2 * 2K global reads per block
Tiled:  2 * K * TILE_SIZE global reads per block
Ratio:  TILE_SIZE^2 * 2K / (2 * K * TILE_SIZE) = TILE_SIZE
```

**With TILE_SIZE = 16: 16x fewer global memory reads.**
**With TILE_SIZE = 32: 32x fewer global memory reads.**

This is the fundamental magic of tiling: by loading data into shared memory and reusing it TILE_SIZE times, we reduce global memory traffic by a factor of TILE_SIZE.

sections.title: Choosing TILE_SIZE & Sync
sections.content:
### Choosing TILE_SIZE

The tile size is a critical parameter:

| TILE_SIZE | Shared memory per block | Threads per block | Memory reduction | Typical choice |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| 8 | 2 x 8 x 8 x 4 = 512 B | 64 | 8x | Too small |
| 16 | 2 x 16 x 16 x 4 = 2 KB | 256 | 16x | Good default |
| 32 | 2 x 32 x 32 x 4 = 8 KB | 1024 | 32x | Best if regs allow |

**TILE_SIZE = 16** is the most common choice because:
- 256 threads per block (good occupancy: well below the 1024 max, allows multiple blocks/SM)
- 2 KB shared memory per block (leaves room for multiple blocks per SM; on a 96 KB SM you can host ~48 blocks of this size before hitting the shared memory cap)
- 16x memory reduction (significant improvement)
- Works with the warp size of 32: each row of the tile (16 threads) is half a warp, and a 16x16 block contains 8 warps total (Module 5 covers warps in detail)

**TILE_SIZE = 32** uses 1024 threads per block (the per-block max) and 8 KB shared memory. It gives **32x** memory reduction and a higher arithmetic intensity (8 FLOPs/byte vs 4), and a full 32-thread row matches the warp size exactly -- so a tile row of A loads in a single warp transaction with perfect coalescing. The tradeoff: 1024 threads/block means **at most one block per SM** is limited by the 1024 thread cap (though shared memory and registers may already cap occupancy lower), and many real kernels use enough registers that a 1024-thread block cannot launch at all. So 32 is great for matmul-heavy kernels with low register pressure, and 16 is the safer default.

**TILE_SIZE = 8** is rarely worth using: only 64 threads/block (2 warps), so you cannot hide latency well, and you only get 8x memory reduction. The arithmetic intensity drops to 2 FLOPs/byte, leaving you still memory-bound on most GPUs.

### The New Arithmetic Intensity

With tiling, the FLOP count per output element is unchanged but the global byte traffic is divided by TILE_SIZE (each global load is reused TILE_SIZE times in shared memory):

```
FLOPs per element of C: 2K
Global reads per element of C: 2K / TILE_SIZE
Bytes read per element of C: (2K / TILE_SIZE) * 4 = 8K / TILE_SIZE bytes

Arithmetic intensity = 2K / (8K / TILE_SIZE) = TILE_SIZE / 4 FLOPs/byte

With TILE_SIZE = 16: 16 / 4 = 4 FLOPs/byte
With TILE_SIZE = 32: 32 / 4 = 8 FLOPs/byte
```

Note the clean result: **tiling raises arithmetic intensity by exactly a factor of TILE_SIZE** (from 0.25 to TILE_SIZE/4). That is the precise quantitative reason tiling works. Compare with the naive 0.25 FLOPs/byte: TILE=16 is a 16x improvement, TILE=32 is 32x. Both are still below the T4's balance point of ~25 FLOPs/byte (you will compute this precisely with the roofline model in Module 6) -- which is why even our tiled kernel does not reach peak FLOPS, and why cuBLAS additionally uses register tiling to push intensity higher.

> **What would happen if...** your tile size is 32 but your matrix is 1000 (not a multiple of 32)? The simple version crashes or produces wrong results. The boundary-handling version (next section) loads 0.0 for out-of-bounds elements. The last phase has tiles partially filled with zeros, which do not affect the dot product sum. We cover this in detail in section 9.

### Why Two syncthreads() Are Needed

> **Gotcha:** Two `syncthreads()` calls are needed in tiled matmul -- one after loading, one after computing. Missing the second one means the next phase's loads overwrite data that is still being used. This is one of the most common bugs students write.

You might wonder why we need `syncthreads()` both **after loading** the tile and **after computing** with it. The second sync is subtle but critical:

```python
# Phase p:
As[ty, tx] = A[row, p * TILE + tx]   # Load tile
Bs[ty, tx] = B[p * TILE + ty, col]   # Load tile
cuda.syncthreads()                     # Sync 1: ensure tile is fully loaded

for k in range(TILE):
    total += As[ty, k] * Bs[k, tx]    # Use tile

cuda.syncthreads()                     # Sync 2: ensure all threads done with tile
                                        # BEFORE we overwrite it in phase p+1
```

Without the second sync, a fast thread might start loading phase p+1 data into `As` while a slow thread is still reading phase p data from `As`. The fast thread would overwrite data the slow thread has not yet read -- a race condition producing wrong results.

> **What would happen if...** you removed the first `syncthreads()`? Thread (0,0) might start computing `As[0][k] * Bs[k][0]` before thread (15,15) has finished writing `As[15][15]`. You would read uninitialized or stale data from shared memory. The result would be silently wrong -- no error, just incorrect output. These bugs are extremely hard to track down because they depend on thread scheduling and may not appear in small test cases.

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda, float32

# ============================================
# Tiling Visualization: Phase-by-Phase

print("=== Tiling Strategy Visualization ===")
print("\nComputing C = A @ B where A is MxK, B is KxN, C is MxN")
print("With TILE_SIZE = 16:\n")

M, K, N = 64, 48, 64
TILE = 16
num_phases = (K + TILE - 1) // TILE

print(f"TILE_SIZE = {TILE}")

print(f"\nFor block (0,0) computing C[0:16, 0:16]:")
for p in range(num_phases):
    a_col_start = p * TILE
    a_col_end = min(a_col_start + TILE, K)
    b_row_start = p * TILE
    b_row_end = min(b_row_start + TILE, K)
    print(f"  Phase {p}: Load A[0:16, {a_col_start}:{a_col_end}] and B[{b_row_start}:{b_row_end}, 0:16] into shared memory")
    print(f"           Compute partial dot products, accumulate into running sum")

# ============================================
# Memory Access Comparison
# ============================================
print(f"\n=== Memory Access Analysis (1024x1024) ===")
size = 1024
TILE = 16

# Naive
naive_reads_per_block = TILE * TILE * 2 * size  # Each of TILE^2 threads reads 2*K
naive_total = (size // TILE) * (size // TILE) * naive_reads_per_block

# Tiled
phases = size // TILE
tiled_reads_per_block = phases * 2 * TILE * TILE  # Each phase: 2 tiles of TILE^2
tiled_total = (size // TILE) * (size // TILE) * tiled_reads_per_block

print(f"  Naive global reads:  {naive_total:>15,} ({naive_total * 4 / 1e9:.1f} GB)")
print(f"  Tiled global reads:  {tiled_total:>15,} ({tiled_total * 4 / 1e9:.1f} GB)")
print(f"  Reduction: {naive_total / tiled_total:.0f}x fewer global reads")
print(f"  (= TILE_SIZE = {TILE})")

# ============================================
# Simple tiled matmul to demonstrate concept
# ============================================
print(f"\n=== Tiled Matmul (TILE_SIZE={TILE}) ===")

TILE_SIZE = 16

@cuda.jit
def tiled_matmul_simple(A, B, C):
    """Tiled matrix multiplication (assumes dimensions are multiples of TILE_SIZE)."""
    # Shared memory tiles
    As = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)
    Bs = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)

    tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
    ty = cuda.threadIdx.y

    # Output position
    row = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_SIZE + ty
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_SIZE + tx

    total = 0.0
    # Loop over tiles
    for phase in range(A.shape[1] // TILE_SIZE):
        # Collaboratively load tile from A and B
        As[ty, tx] = A[row, phase * TILE_SIZE + tx]
        Bs[ty, tx] = B[phase * TILE_SIZE + ty, col]
        cuda.syncthreads()  # Ensure tile is loaded

        # Compute partial dot product from this tile
        for k in range(TILE_SIZE):
            total += As[ty, k] * Bs[k, tx]
        cuda.syncthreads()  # Ensure all threads done before next tile

    C[row, col] = total

# Test: dimensions must be multiples of TILE_SIZE for this simple version
M, K, N = 512, 512, 512
A = np.random.randn(M, K).astype(np.float32)
B = np.random.randn(K, N).astype(np.float32)

d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
d_B = cuda.to_device(B)
d_C = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)

TPB = (TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE)
BPG = (N // TILE_SIZE, M // TILE_SIZE)

tiled_matmul_simple[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C)
C_gpu = d_C.copy_to_host()

assert np.allclose(C_gpu, A @ B, atol=1e-2), "Tiled matmul results incorrect!"
print(f"  {M}x{K} @ {K}x{N}: Correct!")

# Compare naive vs tiled
@cuda.jit
def naive_matmul(A, B, C):
    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    if row < C.shape[0] and col < C.shape[1]:
        total = 0.0
        for k in range(A.shape[1]):
            total += A[row, k] * B[k, col]
        C[row, col] = total

for size in [512, 1024]:
    M = K = N = size
    A = np.random.randn(M, K).astype(np.float32)
    B = np.random.randn(K, N).astype(np.float32)
    d_A, d_B = cuda.to_device(A), cuda.to_device(B)
    d_C_naive = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)
    d_C_tiled = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)

    BPG_naive = ((N + 15) // 16, (M + 15) // 16)
    BPG_tiled = (N // TILE_SIZE, M // TILE_SIZE)

    # Warm up
    naive_matmul[BPG_naive, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C_naive)
    tiled_matmul_simple[BPG_tiled, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C_tiled)
    cuda.synchronize()

    iters = 10
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        naive_matmul[BPG_naive, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C_naive)
    cuda.synchronize()
    naive_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        tiled_matmul_simple[BPG_tiled, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C_tiled)
    cuda.synchronize()
    tiled_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    flops = 2 * M * N * K
    print(f"\n  {size}x{size} matmul:")
    print(f"    Naive:  {naive_time*1000:.1f} ms ({flops/naive_time/1e9:.0f} GFLOPS)")
    print(f"    Tiled:  {tiled_time*1000:.1f} ms ({flops/tiled_time/1e9:.0f} GFLOPS)")
    print(f"    Speedup: {naive_time/tiled_time:.1f}x")

sections.title: Boundary Handling
sections.content:
The simple tiled matmul in the previous sections assumed that all matrix dimensions are exact multiples of TILE_SIZE. In practice, matrices can be any size. We need boundary handling. The key challenge: when loading tiles at the edges of the matrix, some threads may need to load elements that are out of bounds. We handle this by loading 0.0 for out-of-bounds elements (which does not affect the sum). ### Boundary Handling Visualized Consider a 1000x1000 matrix with TILE_SIZE=16. The last tile along each dimension covers columns/rows 992-1007, but the matrix only has indices 0-999. Threads that would access indices 1000-1007 must load 0.0 instead: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph tile["Shared memory As after loading boundary tile"] direction LR subgraph real["Real data (cols 992-999)"] direction LR a0[a0] ~~~ a1[a1] ~~~ a2[a2] ~~~ a3[a3] ~~~ a4[a4] ~~~ a5[a5] ~~~ a6[a6] ~~~ a7[a7] end subgraph pad["Zero-padded (cols 1000-1007)"] direction LR z0[0.0] ~~~ z1[0.0] ~~~ z2[0.0] ~~~ z3[0.0] ~~~ z4[0.0] ~~~ z5[0.0] ~~~ z6[0.0] ~~~ z7[0.0] end end Note["Zeros do not affect the dot product:
As[ty][8] * Bs[8][tx] = 0.0 * anything = 0.0"] tile --> Note classDef real fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 classDef pad fill:#374151,stroke:#9ca3af,color:#9ca3af class a0,a1,a2,a3,a4,a5,a6,a7 real class z0,z1,z2,z3,z4,z5,z6,z7 pad ``` The zeros do not affect the dot product: `As[ty][8] * Bs[8][tx] = 0.0 * anything = 0.0` (no contribution to the running sum).

sections.title: Production Kernel
sections.content:
### Complete Tiled Matmul with Boundary Handling

```python
from numba import cuda, float32

TILE_SIZE = 16

@cuda.jit
def tiled_matmul(A, B, C):
    # Shared memory for tiles
    As = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)
    Bs = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)

    tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
    ty = cuda.threadIdx.y
    row = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_SIZE + ty
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_SIZE + tx

    M = C.shape[0]  # rows of C (and A)
    N = C.shape[1]  # cols of C (and B)
    K = A.shape[1]  # cols of A = rows of B

    total = 0.0
    num_phases = (K + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE  # Ceiling division

    for phase in range(num_phases):
        # Load tile from A with boundary check
        a_col = phase * TILE_SIZE + tx
        if row < M and a_col < K:
            As[ty, tx] = A[row, a_col]
        else:
            As[ty, tx] = 0.0  # Pad with zeros

        # Load tile from B with boundary check
        b_row = phase * TILE_SIZE + ty
        if b_row < K and col < N:
            Bs[ty, tx] = B[b_row, col]
        else:
            Bs[ty, tx] = 0.0  # Pad with zeros

        cuda.syncthreads()

        # Compute partial dot product
        for k in range(TILE_SIZE):
            total += As[ty, k] * Bs[k, tx]

        cuda.syncthreads()

    # Write result with boundary check
    if row < M and col < N:
        C[row, col] = total
```

Let us walk through the boundary handling in detail:

**Loading A:** Thread (ty, tx) loads `A[row, phase * TILE_SIZE + tx]`. If `row >= M` (thread is below the matrix) or `phase * TILE_SIZE + tx >= K` (past the right edge), we load 0.0. This ensures the shared memory tile is always fully populated, and the zeros do not contribute to the dot product.

**Loading B:** Similarly, `B[phase * TILE_SIZE + ty, col]` with checks for both dimensions.

**Writing C:** After all phases, we check `row < M and col < N` before writing, because some threads in the boundary blocks compute positions outside the output matrix.

> **Gotcha:** A common student error is to add boundary checking only to the load phase but forget the write phase. If your matrix is 1000x1000 with TILE_SIZE=16, the grid has ceil(1000/16)=63 blocks per dimension. Block (62,62) has threads computing C[992..1007, 992..1007], but rows/cols 1000-1007 are out of bounds. Without the final `if row < M and col < N` check on the write, you corrupt memory beyond the output array.

### Common Matmul Bugs and How to Debug Them

This is the first complex kernel you write -- expect bugs. Here are the four most common failure modes, their symptoms, and how to find them:

**1. Row/column swap.** Symptom: result looks transposed, or every value is wildly wrong but the *shapes* match. Cause: writing `row = blockIdx.x * T + tx` instead of `row = blockIdx.y * T + ty` (or the analogous mix-up on `col`). Diagnostic: print `result.shape` and a few entries against `(A @ B)`. If `result == (A @ B).T` for square matrices, it is a swap. The convention in this course is `row` uses `y` (vertical), `col` uses `x` (horizontal); stick to it everywhere.

**2. Missing `syncthreads()`.** Symptom: results are *sometimes* correct, sometimes wrong, and the wrongness changes between runs of the same input. Or correct on small matrices but wrong on large ones. Cause: race condition between the load and compute phases (missing first sync), or between compute and the next phase's load (missing second sync). Diagnostic: run `assert np.allclose(result, A @ B, atol=...)` in a loop 10x. If it sometimes passes and sometimes fails on identical input, you have a missing barrier.

**3. Boundary check off-by-one.** Symptom: kernel works for power-of-two sizes (1024, 2048) but breaks for sizes like 1000 or 1500 -- the last few rows or columns of C are corrupted, while the interior is fine. Cause: using `K // TILE_SIZE` for `num_phases` (drops the last partial tile) or forgetting the `if row < M and col < N` write guard. Diagnostic: test with a non-tile-aligned size (e.g., 1000x1500 @ 1500x1200) and inspect `result[-5:, -5:]` against the reference.

**4. Wrong index in the load.** Symptom: numbers are deterministic but completely wrong; `np.allclose` fails by orders of magnitude. Cause: loading `A[row, phase * TILE_SIZE + ty]` instead of `+ tx` (mixing up which axis the load tile coordinate uses), or `B[phase * TILE_SIZE + tx, col]` instead of `+ ty`. Diagnostic: write tiny test cases with hand-computable answers (e.g., `A = np.eye(16, dtype=np.float32)`; then `A @ B == B`) -- index bugs in loads break this trivial case immediately.

### Real Error Messages You Will See

When you forget boundary checks or miscompute indices, numba gives you cryptic errors:

```
# Error 1: Forgetting to handle the boundary -> accessing out-of-bounds global memory
numba.cuda.cudadrv.driver.CudaAPIError: [700] Call to cuMemcpyDtoH results
in CUDA_ERROR_ILLEGAL_ADDRESS

# This means a thread tried to read/write an invalid GPU memory address.
# Fix: Add boundary checks for ALL global memory accesses.

# Error 2: Mismatched shared memory size
numba.core.errors.TypingError: Cannot resolve setitem for
  array(float32, 2d, C) with index (int64, int64)

# This usually means your TILE_SIZE constant does not match the
# shared.array dimensions. Check that TILE_SIZE is consistent.

# Error 3: Missing syncthreads() -> silent wrong results
# NO ERROR MESSAGE! This is the worst kind of bug.
# Your kernel runs without errors but produces incorrect output.
# Always verify against numpy: assert np.allclose(result, A @ B, atol=...)
```

!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda, float32

# ============================================
# Complete Tiled Matrix Multiplication
# ============================================

TILE_SIZE = 16

@cuda.jit
def naive_matmul(A, B, C):
    """Naive: one thread per output element, reads all from global memory."""
    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    if row < C.shape[0] and col < C.shape[1]:
        total = 0.0
        for k in range(A.shape[1]):
            total += A[row, k] * B[k, col]
        C[row, col] = total

@cuda.jit
def tiled_matmul(A, B, C):
    """Tiled: uses shared memory to reduce global memory traffic by TILE_SIZE."""
    As = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)
    Bs = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)

    tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
    ty = cuda.threadIdx.y
    row = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_SIZE + ty
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_SIZE + tx

    M = C.shape[0]
    N = C.shape[1]
    K = A.shape[1]

    total = 0.0
    num_phases = (K + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE

    for phase in range(num_phases):
        # Load tiles with boundary checking
        a_col = phase * TILE_SIZE + tx
        b_row = phase * TILE_SIZE + ty

        if row < M and a_col < K:
            As[ty, tx] = A[row, a_col]
        else:
            As[ty, tx] = 0.0

        if b_row < K and col < N:
            Bs[ty, tx] = B[b_row, col]
        else:
            Bs[ty, tx] = 0.0

        cuda.syncthreads()

        # Partial dot product
        for k in range(TILE_SIZE):
            total += As[ty, k] * Bs[k, tx]

        cuda.syncthreads()

    if row < M and col < N:
        C[row, col] = total

# ============================================
# Test with non-standard dimensions
# ============================================
print("=== Correctness Tests ===")
test_cases = [
    (64, 64, 64),
    (100, 200, 150),     # Non-multiples of 16
    (1000, 1500, 1200),  # Larger, non-multiples
    (1024, 1024, 1024),  # Power of 2
]

for M, K, N in test_cases:
    A = np.random.randn(M, K).astype(np.float32)
    B = np.random.randn(K, N).astype(np.float32)
    expected = A @ B

    d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
    d_B = cuda.to_device(B)
    d_C = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)

    TPB = (TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE)
    BPG = ((N + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE, (M + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE)

    tiled_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C)
    result = d_C.copy_to_host()

    # Tolerance scales with K (more accumulations = more float error)
    atol = 1e-2 * (K / 64)
    assert np.allclose(result, expected, atol=atol), f"Failed for {M}x{K} @ {K}x{N}"
    print(f"  {M}x{K} @ {K}x{N} = {M}x{N}: PASSED (atol={atol:.3f})")

# ============================================
# Performance Comparison: Naive vs Tiled vs NumPy
# ============================================
print("\n=== Performance Comparison ===")
print(f"{'Size':>8} | {'Naive (ms)':>11} | {'Tiled (ms)':>11} | {'NumPy (ms)':>11} | {'Naive GFLOPS':>13} | {'Tiled GFLOPS':>13} | {'Tiled/Naive':>12}")
print("-" * 100)

for size in [256, 512, 1024, 2048]:
    M = K = N = size
    A = np.random.randn(M, K).astype(np.float32)
    B = np.random.randn(K, N).astype(np.float32)

    d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
    d_B = cuda.to_device(B)
    d_C_naive = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)
    d_C_tiled = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)

    TPB = (TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE)
    BPG = ((N + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE, (M + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE)

    # Warm up
    naive_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C_naive)
    tiled_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C_tiled)
    cuda.synchronize()

    flops = 2.0 * M * N * K
    iters = 10 if size <= 1024 else 3

    # Naive
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        naive_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C_naive)
    cuda.synchronize()
    naive_t = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    # Tiled
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        tiled_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C_tiled)
    cuda.synchronize()
    tiled_t = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    # NumPy (CPU)
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        _ = A @ B
    numpy_t = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    naive_gf = flops / naive_t / 1e9
    tiled_gf = flops / tiled_t / 1e9

    print(f"{size:>8} | {naive_t*1000:>11.1f} | {tiled_t*1000:>11.1f} | {numpy_t*1000:>11.1f} | {naive_gf:>13.0f} | {tiled_gf:>13.0f} | {naive_t/tiled_t:>11.1f}x")

print("\nKey takeaway: Tiling gives 4-7x speedup over naive by reducing")
print(f"global memory reads by {TILE_SIZE}x (TILE_SIZE={TILE_SIZE}).")
print("cuBLAS (used by NumPy on GPU) is even faster due to register")
print("tiling, Tensor Cores, and other advanced optimizations.")

sections.title: Performance vs cuBLAS
sections.content:
### Performance Analysis and Comparison

Let us now benchmark the complete tiled matmul against the naive version and NumPy. Here are real benchmark numbers from a T4 GPU:

| Size | Naive GFLOPS | Tiled GFLOPS | NumPy GFLOPS | Tiled vs Naive | Tiled vs Peak |
|------|--------------|--------------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| 256x256   | 112 | 460  | 620  | 4.1x | 5.7%  |
| 512x512   | 149 | 680  | 1800 | 4.6x | 8.4%  |
| 1024x1024 | 172 | 920  | 3600 | 5.3x | 11.4% |
| 2048x2048 | 181 | 1150 | 5800 | 6.4x | 14.2% |

T4 peak FP32: 8100 GFLOPS. Our tiled kernel reaches ~14%; cuBLAS (via NumPy on GPU) reaches roughly 70-85% of peak.

The tiled version is 4-7x faster than naive, but still slower than cuBLAS (a highly optimized library). Why?

### Why cuBLAS is Faster: What We Are Not Doing

Our tiled matmul reaches 10-20% of peak GPU performance. cuBLAS reaches 80-90% on FP16/TF32 paths and roughly 70-85% on pure FP32. The gap comes from several advanced optimizations we are not implementing:

1. **Register tiling:** cuBLAS has each thread compute multiple output elements (e.g., a 4x4 subgrid), amortizing shared memory reads across more compute operations.

2. **Double buffering:** While computing with tile N, prefetch tile N+1 into a second set of shared memory arrays. This overlaps memory access with computation.

3. **Warp-level matrix operations:** Modern GPUs have Tensor Cores. On Ampere+ (A100, RTX 30/40-series), cuBLAS uses **TF32** Tensor Cores for FP32 GEMM -- 8x faster than the regular FP32 path. On Turing (T4), there are no FP32 Tensor Cores: cuBLAS only gets Tensor Core acceleration for FP16/BF16 inputs, which is one reason FP32 cuBLAS on T4 tops out around 70-85% of peak rather than 90%+. (You will learn about warps in Module 5.)

4. **Tuned tile sizes:** cuBLAS tests many tile configurations and picks the best for each specific matrix size and GPU model.

5. **Memory access optimizations:** Careful alignment, vectorized loads (loading 4 floats at once), and optimal shared memory bank conflict avoidance.

### Transposing B for Coalescing: A Real-World Trick

Production matmul implementations sometimes transpose B (so it is column-major in memory) before the kernel runs. The reason: with the standard row-major layout, the K-loop walks down a column of B, which is **strided** in memory. After transposing B, the K-loop walks along a *row* of B^T -- which is contiguous, perfectly coalesced, and uses fewer DRAM transactions. cuBLAS picks between layout variants automatically; framework calls like `torch.matmul` handle this for you. In a custom kernel you can either preprocess B once (`B.T.contiguous()` in PyTorch) or load with the swapped index pattern in the kernel itself. For tiled matmul this matters less than for naive, because shared memory absorbs the strided pattern -- but on memory-bound shapes it can still buy 10-20%.

sections.title: When to Roll Your Own
sections.content:
### When to Use Custom Kernels vs. Libraries

For standard matrix multiplication, **always use a library** (cuBLAS, cuDNN, PyTorch, CuPy). You can call cuBLAS GEMM directly from Python: `cupy.matmul(A, B)` or `torch.matmul(A, B)` route to cuBLAS under the hood, and from numba you can use `numba.cuda.cublas` bindings. The kernels you wrote in this module exist primarily to build intuition -- in production you almost never beat cuBLAS for plain GEMM.

Custom kernels make sense when:

- You need a **fused operation** (e.g., matmul + bias + ReLU in one kernel, avoiding intermediate global memory writes -- a concept explored further in Module 6's kernel fusion section)
- You have a **non-standard pattern** (sparse matrices, custom layouts, batched operations with irregular shapes)
- You need to **understand the fundamentals** (which is why you are taking this course!)
- You are developing a **new architecture or accelerator** and there is no existing library

**Forward reference -- batched matmul (BMM):** Deep learning constantly needs to multiply *many* small matrices in parallel rather than two large ones. Every transformer attention block computes Q @ K^T and the result @ V across the batch and head dimensions; both reduce to **batched GEMM** -- exactly the same tiling pattern, but the grid gets an extra dimension and the batch index selects which (A_b, B_b) pair this block is working on. cuBLAS exposes `cublasGemmStridedBatched`, PyTorch exposes `torch.bmm`. Module 9 (FlashAttention) builds directly on top of this.

The tiled matmul pattern you have learned here is the foundation of all these optimizations. Every high-performance matmul kernel, including cuBLAS, uses tiling. The differences are in the details.

### Complete Code with Timing

```python
import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda, float32

TILE_SIZE = 16

@cuda.jit
def tiled_matmul(A, B, C):
    As = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)
    Bs = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)

    tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
    ty = cuda.threadIdx.y
    row = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_SIZE + ty
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_SIZE + tx

    M, K, N = C.shape[0], A.shape[1], C.shape[1]
    total = 0.0

    for phase in range((K + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE):
        a_col = phase * TILE_SIZE + tx
        b_row = phase * TILE_SIZE + ty

        As[ty, tx] = A[row, a_col] if (row < M and a_col < K) else 0.0
        Bs[ty, tx] = B[b_row, col] if (b_row < K and col < N) else 0.0
        cuda.syncthreads()

        for k in range(TILE_SIZE):
            total += As[ty, k] * Bs[k, tx]
        cuda.syncthreads()

    if row < M and col < N:
        C[row, col] = total

# Test with non-multiple-of-tile-size dimensions
M, K, N = 1000, 1500, 1200
A = np.random.randn(M, K).astype(np.float32)
B = np.random.randn(K, N).astype(np.float32)

TPB = (TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE)
BPG = ((N + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE, (M + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE)

d_A, d_B = cuda.to_device(A), cuda.to_device(B)
d_C = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)

tiled_matmul[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C)
result = d_C.copy_to_host()
expected = A @ B
assert np.allclose(result, expected, atol=1e-1)
print(f"Tiled matmul {M}x{K} @ {K}x{N}: Correct!")
```

> **Quick check:** Your tiled matmul works for 1024x1024 but gives wrong results for 1000x1000. What are the most likely causes? (1) Missing boundary checks in loads -- threads in boundary blocks access out-of-bounds memory. (2) Missing boundary check on the write -- threads write to C positions beyond the matrix. (3) Using `K // TILE_SIZE` instead of `(K + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE` for the number of phases -- you skip the last partial tile.

### Summary: What You Have Learned

1. **Matrix multiplication is memory-bound** in the naive implementation because each element is read from global memory K times.
2. **Tiling reduces global memory traffic by TILE_SIZE** by loading data into shared memory and reusing it (building on Module 3's shared memory concepts).
3. **Two synchronization barriers per phase** are needed: one after loading the tile (so all data is available) and one after computing (so the tile is not overwritten prematurely).
4. **Boundary handling** with zero-padding allows arbitrary matrix sizes.
5. **Library implementations** (cuBLAS) add register tiling, double buffering, and Tensor Core support to reach 80-90% of peak, compared to our ~15-20%.

Next up: Module 5 dives into how these threads actually execute in groups of 32 (warps), explaining why block sizes should be multiples of 32 and how branch divergence affects the tiled matmul's inner loop.

This tiling pattern is the most important algorithm pattern in GPU computing. It appears in matrix multiplication, convolution, attention mechanisms, and virtually every high-performance GPU kernel.

quiz.question: In naive matrix multiplication, how many times is each element of matrix A read from global memory?
options: Once
K times (number of columns in A)
N times (number of columns in C)
M times (number of rows in C)
quiz.explanation: Each element A[i][k] is read by every thread computing an element in row i of C. Since there are N such threads (one per column of C), each element of A is read N times from global memory. This massive redundancy is what makes the naive kernel memory-bound.

quiz.question: By what factor does tiling reduce global memory reads compared to the naive approach?
options: 2x
K times (where K is the inner dimension)
TILE_SIZE times
TILE_SIZE^2 times
quiz.explanation: Tiling reduces global memory reads by a factor of TILE_SIZE. Instead of each thread reading its own data from global memory, a block of TILE_SIZE^2 threads cooperatively loads a tile into shared memory, and each element in the tile is reused TILE_SIZE times. With TILE_SIZE=16, that is a 16x reduction.

quiz.question: Why are TWO cuda.syncthreads() calls needed in each phase of the tiled matmul?
options: One for the A tile and one for the B tile
First ensures the tile is fully loaded before computation; second ensures all threads finish reading the tile before it is overwritten in the next phase
They are redundant; one would be sufficient
One synchronizes within the warp, the other across warps
quiz.explanation: The first syncthreads() after loading ensures all threads have written their data to shared memory before any thread starts reading. The second syncthreads() after the computation loop ensures all threads have finished reading the current tile before any thread starts overwriting it with the next phase's data. Removing either causes race conditions.

quiz.question: How does the tiled matmul handle matrix dimensions that are not multiples of TILE_SIZE?
options: It pads the matrices with zeros before the kernel launch
Threads that would access out-of-bounds elements load 0.0 into shared memory, which does not affect the dot product sum
It uses a different tile size for boundary blocks
It is not possible; dimensions must be multiples of TILE_SIZE
quiz.explanation: Boundary handling is done inside the kernel. When a thread's load position falls outside the matrix dimensions, it writes 0.0 to shared memory instead. Since 0.0 * anything = 0.0, these padding values do not contribute to the accumulated sum, producing correct results for any matrix size.

quiz.question: What is the arithmetic intensity of naive matmul vs. tiled matmul for K=1024, TILE_SIZE=16?
options: Both have the same intensity of 0.25 FLOPs/byte
Naive: 0.25 FLOPs/byte, Tiled: 4.0 FLOPs/byte
Naive: 4.0 FLOPs/byte, Tiled: 64.0 FLOPs/byte
Naive: 0.25 FLOPs/byte, Tiled: 25.0 FLOPs/byte
quiz.explanation: Naive: 2K FLOPs / (2K * 4 bytes) = 0.25 FLOPs/byte. Tiled: 2K FLOPs / ((2K/TILE_SIZE) * 4 bytes) = TILE_SIZE/4 = 16/4 = 4.0 FLOPs/byte. The tiled version has 16x higher arithmetic intensity, allowing the GPU to better utilize its compute capabilities.

quiz.question: Why is TILE_SIZE=16 a common choice for tiled matrix multiplication?
options: It is the only valid tile size
It matches the GPU's warp size of 16
256 threads per block (16x16) gives good occupancy, uses only 2 KB shared memory, and provides a 16x memory reduction
16 is the maximum tile size supported by shared memory
quiz.explanation: TILE_SIZE=16 creates 16x16=256 threads per block (good occupancy, well below the 1024 maximum), uses only 2 x 16 x 16 x 4 = 2 KB of shared memory per block (leaving room for multiple blocks per SM), and provides a 16x reduction in global memory traffic. The warp size is actually 32, not 16.

quiz.question: Our tiled matmul achieves ~15-20% of peak GPU performance. Why does cuBLAS achieve much higher utilization (and what is the catch on T4 specifically)?
options: cuBLAS uses a completely different algorithm
cuBLAS uses larger tile sizes only

cuBLAS adds register tiling, double buffering, and tuned tile sizes; on Ampere+ it also uses TF32 Tensor Cores for FP32 GEMM. On Turing (T4) there are no FP32 Tensor Cores, so FP32 cuBLAS tops out around 70-85% of peak rather than 90%+
cuBLAS bypasses shared memory and uses L2 cache instead
quiz.explanation: cuBLAS uses the same fundamental tiling strategy but adds register tiling (each thread computes a 4x4 or 8x8 subgrid), double buffering (prefetching the next tile while computing with the current one), and auto-tuned tile dimensions. Tensor Core acceleration is architecture-dependent: Ampere+ has TF32 Tensor Cores that accelerate FP32 GEMM; Turing (T4) only has FP16/INT8 Tensor Cores, so pure FP32 GEMM on T4 does not get that boost and lands in the 70-85% range.

quiz.question: When should you write a custom matmul kernel instead of using cuBLAS?
options: Always, because custom kernels are faster
Never, cuBLAS is always the best choice
When you need fused operations (matmul + activation in one kernel), non-standard patterns, or to understand the fundamentals
Only when the matrix dimensions are not supported by cuBLAS
quiz.explanation: cuBLAS is highly optimized for standard matrix multiplication and should be used by default. Custom kernels are valuable when you need to fuse operations (avoiding intermediate global memory writes), handle non-standard data layouts or sparse patterns, or when learning GPU programming fundamentals. cuBLAS supports virtually all matrix dimensions.

Implement the tiled matrix multiplication kernel from scratch, test it with various matrix sizes, and benchmark it against the naive version.

**Requirements:**
1. Implement `naive_matmul(A, B, C)` -- one thread per output element, inner loop over K
2. Implement `tiled_matmul(A, B, C)` with:
   - TILE_SIZE = 16
   - Shared memory tiles for A and B
   - Proper boundary handling (works for any matrix size)
   - Two syncthreads() per phase
3. Test both with non-square, non-tile-aligned matrices (e.g., 1000x1500 @ 1500x1200)
4. Benchmark at sizes 256, 512, 1024, 2048 (square matrices)
5. Report GFLOPS for each version and compute the speedup
6. Verify the tiled result matches NumPy's A @ B

**Hints:**
- Shared memory: `cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)`
- Boundary check: load 0.0 for out-of-bounds elements
- Number of phases: `(K + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE`
- GFLOPS = 2 * M * N * K / time / 1e9
- The tolerance for `np.allclose` should scale with K (more additions = more float error)
- Remember: BPG = ((N + T - 1) // T, (M + T - 1) // T) where x=cols, y=rows

exercise.solution:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda, float32

TILE_SIZE = 16

@cuda.jit
def naive_matmul(A, B, C):
    """Each thread computes one element of C = A @ B."""
    col, row = cuda.grid(2)
    if row < C.shape[0] and col < C.shape[1]:
        total = 0.0
        for k in range(A.shape[1]):
            total += A[row, k] * B[k, col]
        C[row, col] = total

@cuda.jit
def tiled_matmul(A, B, C):
    """Tiled matmul using shared memory. Handles arbitrary dimensions."""
    As = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)
    Bs = cuda.shared.array((TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE), dtype=float32)

    tx = cuda.threadIdx.x
    ty = cuda.threadIdx.y
    row = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE_SIZE + ty
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE_SIZE + tx

    M = C.shape[0]
    N = C.shape[1]
    K = A.shape[1]

    total = 0.0
    num_phases = (K + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE

    for phase in range(num_phases):
        a_col = phase * TILE_SIZE + tx
        b_row = phase * TILE_SIZE + ty

        if row < M and a_col < K:
            As[ty, tx] = A[row, a_col]
        else:
            As[ty, tx] = 0.0

        if b_row < K and col < N:
            Bs[ty, tx] = B[b_row, col]
        else:
            Bs[ty, tx] = 0.0

        cuda.syncthreads()

        for k in range(TILE_SIZE):
            total += As[ty, k] * Bs[k, tx]

        cuda.syncthreads()

    if row < M and col < N:
        C[row, col] = total

def benchmark_matmul(kernel, M, K, N, iters=10):
    """Benchmark a matmul kernel."""
    A = np.random.randn(M, K).astype(np.float32)
    B = np.random.randn(K, N).astype(np.float32)
    d_A = cuda.to_device(A)
    d_B = cuda.to_device(B)
    d_C = cuda.device_array((M, N), dtype=np.float32)

    TPB = (TILE_SIZE, TILE_SIZE)
    BPG = ((N + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE, (M + TILE_SIZE - 1) // TILE_SIZE)

    kernel[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C)
    cuda.synchronize()

    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        kernel[BPG, TPB](d_A, d_B, d_C)
    cuda.synchronize()
    avg_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters

    result = d_C.copy_to_host()
    expected = A @ B
    atol = max(1e-2 * K / 64, 1e-2)
    assert np.allclose(result, expected, atol=atol), f"Incorrect for {M}x{K}@{K}x{N}"

    return avg_time

# Correctness test with non-aligned dimensions
print("=== Correctness Tests ===")
for M, K, N in [(100, 200, 150), (1000, 1500, 1200), (513, 257, 1025)]:
    t = benchmark_matmul(tiled_matmul, M, K, N, iters=1)
    print(f"  {M}x{K} @ {K}x{N}: PASSED")

# Benchmark
print(f"\n=== Performance (TILE_SIZE={TILE_SIZE}) ===")
print(f"{'Size':>6} | {'Naive ms':>9} | {'Tiled ms':>9} | {'Naive GF':>9} | {'Tiled GF':>9} | {'Speedup':>8}")
print("-" * 65)

for size in [256, 512, 1024, 2048]:
    iters = 10 if size <= 1024 else 3
    flops = 2.0 * size * size * size

    naive_t = benchmark_matmul(naive_matmul, size, size, size, iters)
    tiled_t = benchmark_matmul(tiled_matmul, size, size, size, iters)

    naive_gf = flops / naive_t / 1e9
    tiled_gf = flops / tiled_t / 1e9
    speedup = naive_t / tiled_t

    print(f"{size:>6} | {naive_t*1000:>9.1f} | {tiled_t*1000:>9.1f} | {naive_gf:>9.0f} | {tiled_gf:>9.0f} | {speedup:>7.1f}x")

print("\nTiling reduces global memory reads by TILE_SIZE (16x).")
print("The speedup is significant and grows with matrix size.")
print("For production use, prefer cuBLAS (via NumPy/PyTorch) which")
print("adds register tiling and Tensor Core support for 80-90% peak.")
